title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.1 Why Start with Classical Reinforcement Learning?
Reinforcement learning studies how an agent can learn to make decisions through interaction with an environment. Unlike supervised learning, where a model is trained from labeled input-output pairs, reinforcement learning is about sequential action. The learner is not told the correct action at every step. Instead, it acts, observes the consequences, and receives rewards that may be delayed, noisy, or sparse.
This delayed-feedback setting is what makes reinforcement learning both powerful and difficult. A decision that looks bad in the short term may create a better future state. A decision that gives immediate reward may block better long-term outcomes. Because of this, reinforcement learning is not only about prediction. It is about strategy.
At a high level, the field asks a simple question:
How should an agent act so that the total long-term reward is as large as possible?
That question appears in many forms. A game-playing agent chooses moves. A robot chooses control signals. A recommendation system chooses which item to show. A trading system chooses whether to buy, sell, or wait. In each case, the system must act under uncertainty, and each action can influence what happens next.
Deep reinforcement learning receives most of the attention today because it combines neural networks with reinforcement learning objectives. That combination made it possible to learn from high-dimensional observations such as images, and it led to famous results in Atari games, Go, robotics, and large-scale decision systems.
However, deep reinforcement learning is much easier to misunderstand if we skip the classical foundations. DQN is still built on Q-learning. Actor-critic methods still depend on value estimation. Advantage functions still come from the same logic as return estimation and baseline correction. If the foundations are weak, deep methods can look like a collection of engineering tricks rather than a coherent family of ideas.
For that reason, this book begins with:
Only after that do we move into function approximation and deep reinforcement learning.

title: 1  Introduction
section: 1.2 Why Deep Reinforcement Learning Is Hard
text:
1.2 Why Deep Reinforcement Learning Is Hard
The move from classical reinforcement learning to deep reinforcement learning is not just a change of model size. It changes the training dynamics in important ways.
In tabular reinforcement learning, each state or state-action pair can be stored explicitly. Updates are local and often interpretable. In deep reinforcement learning, a neural network shares parameters across many states. This gives us generalization, but it also introduces instability. A single gradient update can change predictions for many states at once. Bootstrapping, off-policy learning, and function approximation can interact in unstable ways. This is one reason techniques such as replay buffers, target networks, normalization, clipping, trust regions, and entropy regularization became so important in practice.
Another difficulty is evaluation. In supervised learning, a validation set often provides a relatively direct signal of progress. In reinforcement learning, performance can vary greatly across random seeds, reward scales, exploration strategies, environment wrappers, and implementation details. Two systems that look similar on paper may behave very differently during training.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.3 How to Read This Book
This book is organized as a gradual progression.
The first part builds the conceptual and mathematical foundations of reinforcement learning. The second part bridges classical methods and deep methods. The third part focuses on practical deep reinforcement learning, including stability, debugging, and implementation judgment.
When reading, it helps to keep three questions in mind:

What is being estimated?
What is being improved?
What assumptions make the method work?

Those three questions are useful almost everywhere in reinforcement learning. A value-based method estimates values and improves a policy indirectly. A policy-based method improves the policy directly but may still estimate returns or advantages. An actor-critic method does both. If you keep track of those roles, the algorithm landscape becomes much easier to organize.

title: 1  Introduction
section: 1.4 A Note on the Example Code
text:
1.4 A Note on the Example Code
The main code path for this book is the maintained examples/ directory. That is the codebase the chapters are primarily written against. It contains the tabular examples, the function-approximation bridge examples, the maintained PyTorch DQN path, and the current policy-gradient baseline used by the project.
The repository also contains older reinforcement learning code in machine_learning_examples/. Those files still have value, especially when an older implementation exposes an algorithmic skeleton clearly. But the software stack has changed. Some of those files assume older Gym APIs, TensorFlow 1.x, or older training conventions. In this book, we will use such material mainly as conceptual anchors:

To illustrate the shape of an algorithm.
To compare tabular and neural approaches.
To discuss how modern implementations differ.

Whenever an implementation detail is outdated, the text will say so clearly rather than presenting old patterns as current best practice. The general rule is simple:

examples/ is the maintained code path to read with the book.
machine_learning_examples/ is background material to consult selectively.

By the end of the book, the reader should not only know what major reinforcement learning algorithms do, but also understand why they were developed, when they fail, and how they connect to the broader logic of sequential decision-making.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.1 Exploration and Exploitation
Prediction methods estimate how good a policy is. Control methods go one step further: they try to find a good policy.
In reinforcement learning, this usually means that the agent must both learn and act at the same time. It cannot wait until all uncertainty disappears. It must make decisions while its estimates are still imperfect. That makes control fundamentally harder than pure policy evaluation.
This chapter introduces the basic logic of classical model-free control. The key themes are exploration, action-value estimation, and iterative policy improvement through experience.
One of the first problems in control is the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
If the agent always chooses the action that currently looks best, it may miss better actions that have not been tried enough. If it explores too much, it may waste reward on poor choices and never settle into strong behavior.
This trade-off is not a minor technical detail. It is one of the central design problems of reinforcement learning.
Many classical methods begin with a simple exploration rule such as (\(\epsilon\))-greedy action selection:
This rule is crude, but it is easy to implement and often works well enough in small problems. It also appears in many toy implementations, including classic Q-learning examples.
In the maintained codebase, the simplest places to see this idea are:
Both rely on an \(\epsilon\)-greedy action selector in examples/tabular/control_utils.py. This is a good example of a principle worth carrying from theory into code: exploration is not an optional outer wrapper around learning. It is part of what determines the data distribution, and therefore part of what the agent learns.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.2 Action-Value Control
For control, action values are often more useful than state values. If we estimate (Q(s, a)), then we can select actions directly by comparing action values within the current state.
This is the basic value-based control idea:

Estimate action values.
Choose actions using those estimates.
Update the estimates from experience.
Repeat.

Once action values become accurate enough, greedy action selection can define a strong policy.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.3 On-Policy and Off-Policy Learning
Before introducing SARSA and Q-learning, we need one important distinction.
An on-policy method learns about the policy it is currently using to act. An off-policy method learns about one policy while following another.
This distinction matters because the behavior policy may include exploration, while the target policy may be greedy or nearly greedy. Off-policy methods are often more flexible, but they can also become less stable, especially once function approximation is introduced.
SARSA is the classical on-policy example. Q-learning is the classical off-policy example.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.4 SARSA
SARSA is named after the sequence of variables used in its update:
\[ (S_t, A_t, R\_{t+1}, S\_{t+1}, A\_{t+1}) \]
The update rule is:
\[ Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)\right]\]
This is an on-policy update because the next action (A_{t+1}) is the action actually chosen by the current behavior policy, which may include exploration.
That detail has an important consequence. SARSA learns the value of behaving according to the exploration-aware policy currently in use. If exploratory actions can be dangerous, SARSA may learn more conservative behavior than a purely greedy method.
This makes SARSA especially useful for understanding how learning depends not only on the update rule, but also on the behavior policy generating the data.
The maintained implementation is:

examples/tabular/sarsa.py

If you read run_sarsa(...), the most important line conceptually is the one that selects next_action from the current exploratory policy before building the TD target. That is the code-level meaning of “on-policy” in this chapter. The target is tied to what the agent actually does next, not to a hypothetical greedy action it did not take.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.5 Q-Learning
Q-learning uses a different target:
\[ Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)\right]\]
The max over next actions means the update targets the value of the greedy action, regardless of which action is actually taken next by the behavior policy. That is why Q-learning is off-policy.
Conceptually, Q-learning says:
Act with exploration if needed, but learn as if you were going to behave greedily from the next state onward.
This is a powerful idea. It allows exploratory behavior to collect data while still pushing estimates toward the greedy optimal action-value function (Q^*).
Q-learning is one of the most influential algorithms in reinforcement learning. Its basic structure later reappears in DQN, where the tabular action-value table is replaced by a neural network.
The maintained implementation is:

examples/tabular/q_learning.py

Here the code-level contrast with SARSA is sharp. In run_q_learning(...), the target uses max(q_table[next_state].values()) rather than the value of the next exploratory action actually chosen. That single difference is the operational meaning of “off-policy” in this setting.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.6 SARSA vs. Q-Learning
SARSA and Q-learning often look very similar in code. Both update action-value estimates from one-step transitions. Both are easy to implement in tabular environments. Both can use ()-greedy exploration.
But their learning targets differ:

SARSA uses the next action actually chosen by the current policy.
Q-learning uses the best estimated next action, whether or not it is chosen.

This difference can lead to meaningfully different behavior. In risky environments, SARSA may learn safer policies because it accounts for the exploratory actions that the agent really takes. Q-learning may learn more aggressive policies because it evaluates the greedy target rather than the exploratory behavior policy.
In this project, both maintained scripts use a negative-grid variant with step cost. That is a sensible foundation example because it makes the behavioral difference easier to see. The point is not only whether the final policy reaches the goal. The point is how the learning target changes the kind of path the agent prefers while exploration is still part of the process.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.7 Convergence and Practical Assumptions
In finite tabular settings, classical convergence results are available under assumptions such as:

All relevant state-action pairs are visited sufficiently often.
Learning rates are chosen appropriately.
Exploration does not vanish too early.

These assumptions matter because reinforcement learning is only as good as the data it sees. If the agent never explores a useful action, it cannot learn its value.
At the same time, convergence guarantees in the tabular case should not create false confidence about larger problems. Once we move to function approximation and neural networks, many clean guarantees become weaker or disappear entirely.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.8 A Note on Historical Implementations
Many older reinforcement learning tutorials implement Q-learning with small lookup tables, simple ()-greedy exploration, and lightweight loops over episodes. Those examples are still excellent for understanding the algorithmic core.
For example, a compact tabular Q-learning implementation usually contains the following ingredients:

A table storing (Q(s, a)).
An ()-greedy action selector.
A one-step TD update toward a bootstrap target.
Repetition over many episodes.

That structure is still worth studying. The main limitation is not the logic of the algorithm, but the scale of the representation. Once state spaces become large or continuous, lookup tables stop being practical.
In the maintained code for this book, that compact structure is still present, just cleaned up and reorganized:

examples/tabular/control_utils.py
examples/tabular/sarsa.py
examples/tabular/q_learning.py

These files are deliberately small enough that you can trace the entire control loop from exploration to update target to policy extraction without jumping across a large framework.

title: 6  Classical Control Methods
text:
6.9 Why Classical Control Still Matters
Classical control methods are not just historical stepping stones. They teach several durable lessons:

Value estimation and action selection are tightly linked.
Exploration strategy changes what the agent learns.
On-policy and off-policy learning are meaningfully different.
Bootstrapping can support efficient control.

These lessons carry directly into deep reinforcement learning. DQN inherits the off-policy value-learning structure of Q-learning. Policy-gradient and actor-critic methods respond, in part, to the limitations of value-based control in large or continuous spaces.
The next chapter makes the bridge explicit by moving from tabular methods to function approximation.

title: From Reinforcement Learning Foundations to Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
Preface
This book is an English writing project and a technical learning project at the same time. It is designed for readers who want to build a clean path from the foundations of reinforcement learning to the main ideas of deep reinforcement learning.
Many reinforcement learning resources fall into one of two extremes. Some are mathematically precise but hard to connect to implementation. Others are implementation-driven but move too quickly, leaving the reader with code that works without a clear understanding of why it works. This book tries to stay in the middle. We will begin with the core concepts, then move to classical algorithms, and finally reach deep reinforcement learning methods that rely on function approximation and neural networks.
The main code path used in this book now lives in examples/. That directory is the maintained companion to the text. It contains the tabular examples, function-approximation examples, the PyTorch DQN family, and the currently recommended policy-gradient baseline used in the project.
The repository also still contains older reinforcement learning material in machine_learning_examples/. Those files remain useful as historical reference points, but they are no longer the primary code path of the book. Some of them reflect an earlier software ecosystem, including older versions of Gym and TensorFlow. For that reason, this book treats them as background material rather than as the main implementations readers should follow.
The goal of the draft is not to present the newest benchmark tricks first. Instead, the goal is to build understanding in the right order:

Learn the language of reinforcement learning.
Understand how agents estimate value and improve behavior.
See why tabular methods stop scaling.
Understand how deep learning changes the picture.
Develop enough practical judgment to train and debug DRL systems responsibly.

If you are using this book as an English exercise, that is a strength rather than a distraction. Reinforcement learning already requires careful reading. Writing and reading it in English encourages precision with technical terms such as policy, return, state value, action value, bootstrapping, and off-policy learning. Over time, these terms become part of your working vocabulary.
This book is written as a foundation text rather than a benchmark handbook. The emphasis is on understanding the main objects, equations, and algorithmic patterns clearly enough that code and experiments can be interpreted honestly. Where the repository uses a self-implemented reference, the text will say so. Where the repository relies on a mature third-party implementation for a more reliable baseline, the text will say that too.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.1 Sequential Decision-Making
Reinforcement learning is a framework for learning through interaction. An agent observes a situation, chooses an action, receives a reward, and moves to a new situation. Over time, it tries to improve its decisions so that the total reward it collects becomes as large as possible.
This description sounds simple, but it hides an important difference from other machine learning settings. In supervised learning, the correct answer is usually available in the training data. In reinforcement learning, the agent often has to discover good behavior by trial and error. It must balance two conflicting goals:
That tension between exploitation and exploration is one of the defining features of the field.
The central object in reinforcement learning is not a single prediction but a sequence of decisions. A good action is not simply one that gives immediate reward. It is an action that contributes to good long-term outcomes.
This is why the same action can be good in one context and bad in another. For example, in a game, sacrificing a piece may look costly in the short term but lead to a winning position later. In navigation, taking a longer route may avoid a dead end. In robotics, a stabilizing action may temporarily reduce speed while improving future control.
Reinforcement learning formalizes these problems by focusing on trajectories:
\[
S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, S_2, \dots
\]
At each time step, the agent is in a state \(S_t\), chooses an action \(A_t\), receives a reward \(R_{t+1}\), and transitions to a new state \(S_{t+1}\). The objective is to choose actions so that the long-term return is large.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.2 The Agent and the Environment
The agent is the learner or decision-maker. The environment is everything outside the agent that responds to its actions. This distinction is conceptual rather than physical. In a board game, the environment includes the game rules and board configuration. In robotic control, the environment may include the robot body, the external world, sensors, and dynamics not directly controlled by the learner.
The interaction loop usually looks like this:

The environment presents a state to the agent.
The agent chooses an action according to its current policy.
The environment returns a reward and a next state.
The agent updates its internal estimates or policy.

This loop may happen a few times in a toy problem or millions of times in a large-scale training run.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.3 States, Actions, and Rewards
A state is the information used to describe the current situation. An action is a choice available to the agent. A reward is a scalar signal that evaluates the immediate outcome of an action.
It is important not to confuse reward with objective. Reward is the training signal provided at each step. The objective is usually the expected sum of rewards over time. A reward can be sparse, delayed, shaped, noisy, or even misleading. Much of reinforcement learning becomes difficult when the reward does not immediately reveal which behavior is truly good.
The quality of the state representation also matters. If the state omits critical information, the problem becomes harder because the agent cannot tell apart situations that require different behavior. This is one motivation for later deep reinforcement learning methods, where learned representations become part of the solution.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.4 Policies and Value Functions
A policy tells the agent how to act. We often write a policy as \(\pi(a \mid s)\), the probability of choosing action \(a\) in state \(s\). A deterministic policy always chooses the same action for a given state. A stochastic policy defines a distribution over actions.
While a policy specifies behavior, a value function measures how good states or actions are under a policy. Two common value functions are:

The state-value function \(V^\pi(s)\), which measures the expected return starting from state \(s\) and following policy \(\pi\).
The action-value function \(Q^\pi(s, a)\), which measures the expected return after taking action \(a\) in state \(s\) and then following policy \(\pi\).

These objects are central because many algorithms either estimate value functions directly or use them to improve a policy.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.5 Return and Discounting
The return is the total reward accumulated over time. A common definition is the discounted return:
\[
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots
\]
where \(0 \le \gamma \le 1\) is the discount factor.
The discount factor controls how much the agent cares about the future. A small \(\gamma\) makes the agent short-sighted. A large \(\gamma\) places more weight on long-term consequences. Discounting is useful for both mathematical and practical reasons. It can ensure that infinite sums remain finite, and it can encode a preference for earlier rewards over later ones.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.6 Episodes and Continuing Tasks
Some reinforcement learning problems have natural endings. A game ends when one player wins. A robot navigation task may end when the goal is reached. These are episodic tasks.
Other problems continue indefinitely, such as recommendation systems or certain control problems. These are often treated as continuing tasks. The difference matters because it affects how we define returns, evaluate performance, and structure training.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.7 Model-Based and Model-Free Learning
An agent is model-based if it has access to, or learns, a model of the environment dynamics. That means it can predict how actions change states and rewards. An agent is model-free if it learns behavior or value estimates directly from experience without explicitly learning the transition model.
This book begins mostly with model-free learning, because that path leads naturally into modern deep reinforcement learning. Still, model-based ideas remain important, especially when sample efficiency matters.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
text:
2.8 Why Reinforcement Learning Matters
Reinforcement learning matters because many real-world problems involve decisions, feedback, uncertainty, and long-term consequences. The framework gives us a language for these problems even when perfect solutions are impossible.
At the same time, reinforcement learning should not be treated as a universal hammer. It is often hard to train, expensive in data, and sensitive to problem design. A major part of real expertise is knowing when reinforcement learning is appropriate and when a simpler method would be better.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.1 The Markov Property
The agent-environment framework gives us intuition, but we still need a mathematical structure that makes reinforcement learning precise. The standard framework is the Markov decision process (MDP). It provides a clean way to describe states, actions, rewards, transitions, and long-term objectives.
Many reinforcement learning algorithms can be understood as different ways of solving, approximating, or learning within an MDP.
The defining assumption behind an MDP is the Markov property. Informally, it says that the future depends on the present state, not on the full past history, once the present state is known.
In words:
The current state contains all information needed for predicting the next step.
This does not mean the past is irrelevant in general. It means the state has already summarized the relevant parts of the past. If the state representation fails to do that, then the process is not fully Markov from the agent’s point of view.
This point is important in practice. Many real environments are only approximately Markov. Partial observability, noisy sensors, hidden variables, and missing context can all break the assumption. Deep reinforcement learning often deals with this by learning richer representations, stacking observations, or using recurrent models.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.2 The MDP Components
An MDP is often described by the tuple:
\[
(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)
\]
where:

\(\mathcal{S}\) is the set of states.
\(\mathcal{A}\) is the set of actions.
\(P\) is the transition dynamics.
\(R\) is the reward function.
\(\gamma\) is the discount factor.

The transition dynamics tell us how the environment changes after an action. More formally, we may write:
\[
P(s', r \mid s, a)
\]
which means the probability of moving to state \(s'\) and receiving reward \(r\) after taking action \(a\) in state \(s\).
This form captures both uncertainty in the next state and uncertainty in the reward.
The abstraction may seem very mathematical at first, but in code it usually appears as a small number of concrete data structures.
In the maintained tabular examples for this project, the cleanest place to see this is:

examples/tabular/gridworld.py

That file makes the MDP components explicit:

States are grid coordinates such as (row, col).
Actions are symbols such as "U", "D", "L", and "R".
Rewards are stored in dictionaries keyed by states.
Available actions are stored in dictionaries keyed by states.
The windy variant stores transition probabilities explicitly in transition_probs.

This is a useful point pedagogically. Before reinforcement learning becomes deep or statistical, an MDP is simply a structured description of what can happen next and what reward is attached to it.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.3 Policies Inside an MDP
Once an MDP is defined, the agent still needs a policy. A policy determines how actions are selected in each state. If \(\pi\) is a policy, then:
\[
\pi(a \mid s)
\]
is the probability that the agent chooses action \(a\) in state \(s\).
The policy and the environment together induce a distribution over trajectories. That is why reinforcement learning is fundamentally probabilistic even when the environment or policy is deterministic in special cases.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.4 Returns and Objectives
The main objective in an MDP is usually to maximize expected return. Starting at time \(t\), the discounted return is:
\[
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
\]
This equation formalizes the idea that current actions should be judged by their future consequences, not only by immediate rewards.
There are several reasons discounting is useful:

It gives more weight to near-term rewards.
It often makes infinite-horizon sums well-defined.
It can reduce sensitivity to very distant uncertainty.

In episodic tasks, the return may terminate naturally at the end of an episode. In continuing tasks, discounting often plays a larger role.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.5 State-Value and Action-Value Functions
Value functions describe expected future return under a policy. They translate the long-term objective into quantities that can be estimated and improved.
The state-value function is:
\[
V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s]
\]
It tells us how good it is to be in state \(s\) if we continue to follow policy \(\pi\).
The action-value function is:
\[
Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]
\]
It tells us how good it is to take action \(a\) in state \(s\) and then follow policy \(\pi\).
These functions are central because they support both evaluation and control. If we know which states and actions have high long-term value, we can improve decisions accordingly.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.6 Bellman Equations
One of the most important ideas in reinforcement learning is that value functions satisfy recursive relationships. This is the core insight behind the Bellman equations.
For the state-value function:
\[
V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} P(s', r \mid s, a) \left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right]
\]
This equation says that the value of a state equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state.
Similarly, the action-value function satisfies:
\[
Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} P(s', r \mid s, a) \left[r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^\pi(s', a')\right]
\]
These recursive forms are powerful because they allow value estimation to be built from local consistency conditions rather than from full trajectory enumeration.
In this repository, that recursive structure is not hidden behind a framework. It is written out directly in the tabular examples. The most transparent places to inspect are:

examples/tabular/policy_evaluation.py
examples/tabular/value_iteration.py

Those files are worth reading slowly. The Bellman equations are not only identities on paper. In code, they become loops over states, actions, and possible next states. The theory says a value should satisfy a recursive consistency relation. The implementation turns that statement into repeated sweeps until the estimates stop moving very much.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
text:
3.7 Optimal Value Functions
So far, the value functions have been defined relative to a fixed policy \(\pi\). But reinforcement learning usually aims to find the best possible policy.
The optimal state-value function is:
\[
V^*(s) = \max_\pi V^\pi(s)
\]
and the optimal action-value function is:
\[
Q^*(s, a) = \max_\pi Q^\pi(s, a)
\]
These optimal value functions also satisfy Bellman equations. For example:
\[
V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V^*(s')\right]
\]
and
\[
Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')\right]
\]
These equations replace the policy expectation with a maximization over actions. That single change leads directly to many control algorithms, including Q-learning and DQN.
That single change is easy to underestimate. In fact, it is one of the most important transitions in the whole book. When a policy expectation is replaced by a maximization, the problem changes from evaluating a given behavior to searching for the best behavior.
You can already see this shift in the maintained code before any learning from data begins:

examples/tabular/policy_iteration.py
examples/tabular/value_iteration.py

Then later, in the learning chapters, the same structural change reappears in:

examples/tabular/q_learning.py
examples/deep_rl/dqn_cartpole.py

So the Bellman optimality equation should not be read here as an isolated theorem. It is the mathematical ancestor of the greedy bootstrap target used much later in Q-learning and DQN.

title: 3  Markov Decision Processes
section: 3.8 Why MDPs Matter for Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
3.8 Why MDPs Matter for Deep Reinforcement Learning
At first glance, deep reinforcement learning may seem far away from the clean equations in this chapter. In practice, however, the deep part mostly changes how value functions, policies, or models are represented. It does not change the core logic of the problem.
DQN still tries to approximate an action-value function. Policy gradient methods still optimize a policy in an MDP. Actor-critic methods still rely on value estimates defined through expected return. Even advanced algorithms remain built on the same foundational objects.
This is why MDPs matter. They provide the conceptual skeleton of the entire field.
For this reason, it is worth returning to the MDP language repeatedly while reading later chapters. Even when the code starts to use replay buffers, neural networks, target networks, or third-party training libraries, the foundational questions do not change:

What is the state?
What are the available actions?
What is the reward signal?
What return is being optimized?
Where is the Bellman or policy objective appearing in the update?

If those questions are kept visible, the later algorithms stay interpretable. If they are forgotten, deep reinforcement learning can easily collapse into a collection of names and engineering recipes.
The next chapter studies dynamic programming, which assumes the environment model is known and shows how value functions can be evaluated and improved systematically.

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.1 Monte Carlo Methods
Dynamic programming assumes that the environment model is known. In many realistic problems, that assumption is too strong. The agent does not know the transition probabilities in advance, and it may not know the reward structure either. What it does have is experience.
Monte Carlo methods and temporal-difference learning answer the same broad question:
How can an agent learn value estimates directly from sampled interaction?
These two families of methods are fundamental. They show how reinforcement learning can move from planning with a known model to learning from data.
Monte Carlo methods learn from complete episodes. The core idea is straightforward:
If a state \(s\) appears in an episode, we can estimate its value by averaging the returns observed after visiting that state. Over many episodes, these averages can converge to the true value under the policy being followed.
This approach has an appealing simplicity. It does not require a model, and it does not bootstrap from current estimates. It waits until the return is fully observed.
For example, if the return following a visit to state \(s\) is \(G_t\), then a Monte Carlo estimate of the state value may be updated toward that sampled return:
\[
V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \left(G_t - V(s)\right)
\]
This looks very similar to later update rules. The key difference lies in the target. In Monte Carlo learning, the target is a full sampled return.
In the maintained code for this book, the cleanest tabular Monte Carlo reference is:
The important thing to notice in that file is not just that it samples episodes. It also stores complete trajectories and then walks backward through them to compute returns. That backward pass makes the full-return target explicit in code.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
Run an episode.
Observe the return that actually occurred.
Use that return as a target for value estimation.








examples/tabular/monte_carlo_prediction.py
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.2 First-Visit and Every-Visit Monte Carlo
There are different ways to count returns for a state within an episode.
In first-visit Monte Carlo, only the first occurrence of a state in an episode is used for updating that state’s estimate. In every-visit Monte Carlo, every occurrence is used.
Both approaches can work. The difference is mostly about how samples are collected and correlated. In practice, the main idea remains the same: use complete returns from actual trajectories.
The maintained example currently uses a first-visit style update. In run_first_visit_mc_prediction(...), the code checks whether the current state has already appeared earlier in the same episode before committing the update. That one implementation detail is the practical difference between first-visit and every-visit Monte Carlo.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
section: 5.3 Strengths and Weaknesses of Monte Carlo Learning
text:
5.3 Strengths and Weaknesses of Monte Carlo Learning
Monte Carlo methods have several strengths:

They are conceptually simple.
They do not require a model.
They use unbiased sampled returns as targets.

But they also have limitations:

They require episodes to terminate, or at least to be truncated in a meaningful way.
They may learn slowly when episodes are long.
Their return estimates can have high variance.

That high variance is important. If rewards are delayed or noisy, the complete return can fluctuate greatly from one episode to the next. This makes value estimation unstable or slow.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.4 Temporal-Difference Learning
Temporal-difference learning, usually abbreviated TD learning, takes a different approach. Instead of waiting for the full return, it updates estimates using partially observed experience plus current value estimates.
The simplest case is TD(0):
\[
V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left(R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\right)
\]
This update uses a bootstrapped target:
\[
R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})
\]
Rather than waiting until the end of the episode, TD learning says:
Use the immediate reward plus the current estimate of the next state as a target right now.
This idea is one of the deepest in reinforcement learning. The target is not a full observed outcome. It is part observation, part estimate.
In the maintained code, the cleanest TD(0) example is:

examples/tabular/td0_prediction.py

The key function is run_td0_prediction(...). If you compare it with the Monte Carlo file, the contrast is instructive. The Monte Carlo script stores a whole episode and updates later. The TD script updates immediately after each transition. That single structural difference captures a large part of the conceptual gap between Monte Carlo and TD learning.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.5 The TD Error
The quantity
\[
\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)
\]
is called the temporal-difference error.
It measures how surprising the transition was relative to the current value estimate. If the outcome was better than expected, the TD error is positive and the estimate should increase. If the outcome was worse than expected, the TD error is negative and the estimate should decrease.
This error signal later becomes important far beyond basic prediction. Actor-critic methods use related ideas. Advantage estimation is closely connected to it. Many practical deep reinforcement learning algorithms still depend on TD-style targets.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.6 Monte Carlo vs. Temporal-Difference Learning
Monte Carlo and TD methods both learn from experience, but they make different trade-offs.
Monte Carlo methods:

Wait for full returns.
Do not bootstrap.
Can have high variance.
Are naturally episodic.

Temporal-difference methods:

Update after each step.
Bootstrap from current estimates.
Usually have lower variance.
Can work naturally in continuing tasks.

A common summary is:
Monte Carlo methods wait longer but use more complete targets. TD methods learn earlier but rely on estimates that may themselves be wrong.
This is a bias-variance trade-off. Monte Carlo targets are often less biased but more variable. TD targets are often more biased but less variable and more data-efficient.
That trade-off is not merely theoretical. You can see it in the code organization of the maintained examples:

examples/tabular/monte_carlo_prediction.py
examples/tabular/td0_prediction.py

The Monte Carlo script has to wait for trajectory completion and then reconstruct returns. The TD script can learn online, one transition at a time. The code itself shows why TD methods tend to feel more incremental and more data-efficient in practice.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.7 Bootstrapping as a Core Idea
Bootstrapping means updating an estimate using another estimate. This may seem risky at first, because errors can propagate. But it is also what makes learning efficient. Instead of waiting for full trajectories, the agent can improve estimates online, one transition at a time.
Bootstrapping is also one of the reasons reinforcement learning can become unstable when combined with function approximation. Later in the book, when we study DQN and actor-critic methods, we will see that bootstrapping remains powerful but must be handled carefully.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
section: 5.8 Eligibility Traces and the Space Between MC and TD
text:
5.8 Eligibility Traces and the Space Between MC and TD
Monte Carlo and TD(0) are often presented as separate families, but they are really endpoints of a spectrum. Methods based on eligibility traces, such as TD(\(\lambda\)), interpolate between them.
When \(\lambda\) is closer to 1, the method behaves more like Monte Carlo, relying more on long returns. When \(\lambda\) is closer to 0, it behaves more like TD(0), relying more on one-step bootstrapping.
This spectrum is important because it shows that reinforcement learning methods are often connected by design trade-offs rather than by sharp boundaries.
This book does not yet maintain a full TD(\(\lambda\)) code path in examples/, so the point here should be understood conceptually. It is included because it explains why Monte Carlo and TD(0) should not be thought of as isolated islands. They are endpoints of a family of return-construction choices.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning
text:
5.9 From Prediction to Control
So far, we have mostly discussed value estimation for a fixed policy. But reinforcement learning is usually about control, not just prediction. We want the agent to improve its behavior while learning.
That requires one more step:
The agent must use value estimates to choose better actions.
The next chapter studies this transition. We will move from value prediction to classical control methods, including exploration strategies, SARSA, and Q-learning.
crumbs: 5  Monte Carlo Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
7.1 Why Tabular Methods Stop Scaling
By this point, we have seen several core reinforcement learning ideas in their tabular form. We estimated values, improved policies, compared Monte Carlo and temporal-difference learning, and studied classical control methods such as SARSA and Q-learning.
These methods are conceptually important, but they face a serious limitation:
They do not scale well when the state space becomes large, high-dimensional, or continuous.
This chapter explains why that limitation appears, why function approximation becomes necessary, and how this transition leads directly into deep reinforcement learning.
In a tabular method, the agent stores a separate value for each state or each state-action pair. This works well in small environments such as grid worlds, toy games, or discretized control tasks.
But many realistic problems do not have small state spaces.
Consider a robotic control task with continuous position and velocity variables. Consider a video game where the state is an image. Consider any problem where the observation includes dozens or hundreds of features. In these settings, we cannot expect to visit every possible state enough times to learn a reliable table entry.
There are several related problems:
This last point is especially important. In tabular learning, each state is treated as isolated. Learning that one state is valuable tells us nothing directly about nearby or similar states. That becomes extremely inefficient in large environments.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
The number of states may be enormous.
The state space may be continuous rather than discrete.
Similar states should share information, but a lookup table cannot generalize.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
7.2 Generalization as the Key Motivation
Function approximation solves this problem by replacing explicit tables with parameterized models.
Instead of storing one independent value for each state, we define a function such as:
\[
\hat{V}(s; w)
\]
or
\[
\hat{Q}(s, a; w)
\]
where \(w\) denotes learnable parameters.
Now the agent no longer memorizes values one state at a time. Instead, it learns a mapping from states or state-action pairs to predicted values. Similar inputs can produce similar outputs, so information can generalize across related situations.
This shift changes the entire learning problem. The goal is no longer just to fill in a table. The goal is to learn a useful approximation of the value function or policy over a large input space.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
7.3 Linear Function Approximation
A natural first step is linear approximation. Suppose we extract a feature vector \(x(s)\) from state \(s\). Then we may write:
\[
\hat{V}(s; w) = w^\top x(s)
\]
For action values, we may use features of state-action pairs:
\[
\hat{Q}(s, a; w) = w^\top x(s, a)
\]
This looks simple, but it already changes the learning behavior in an important way. Updating the parameter vector \(w\) changes the prediction for many states at once. This is the source of generalization.
Older reinforcement learning examples often illustrate this step with hand-designed features, radial basis function expansions, or other shallow approximators. Those examples remain useful because they expose the transition from tabular storage to shared parameterization very clearly.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
section: 7.4 A Conceptual Bridge from Old Examples
text:
7.4 A Conceptual Bridge from Old Examples
In this repository, the maintained bridge examples now live in:

examples/approximation/cartpole_linear_q.py
examples/approximation/mountaincar_linear_q.py

These scripts use explicit feature engineering together with a shallow linear model to approximate action values. Although they are much smaller than modern deep RL systems, their conceptual structure is exactly the bridge we need:

Represent a state-action pair as a feature vector.
Predict a scalar value from that representation.
Build a bootstrap target from reward plus an estimate of the next state’s best action value.
Update the model parameters toward that target.

That structure is the real bridge. Deep reinforcement learning keeps the same high-level pattern, but replaces hand-designed features and shallow models with neural networks.
At the same time, these maintained examples should not be interpreted as equally strong engineering baselines. The CartPole approximation example is currently the more stable of the two. The MountainCar version is useful conceptually, but remains more sensitive to feature choice and hyperparameters.
This distinction is important for how this chapter should be read. The role of these files is not mainly to provide the strongest benchmark results in the repository. Their role is to let the reader see, in a small and inspectable form, what changes when a lookup table becomes a parameterized function:

the value estimate becomes a prediction from features,
a single update changes many states at once,
and stability becomes partly a representation problem rather than only a sampling problem.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
section: 7.5 The Promise and Risk of Approximation
text:
7.5 The Promise and Risk of Approximation
Function approximation gives us scalability and generalization, but it also introduces new difficulties.
In a table, an update only changes one entry. With approximation, a single update can affect many predictions at once. This means generalization can help, but it can also cause interference. Improving one region of the state space may accidentally damage estimates elsewhere.
This leads to a new tension:

Shared parameters make learning more efficient.
Shared parameters also make learning less local and potentially less stable.

That trade-off becomes one of the defining difficulties of deep reinforcement learning.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
7.6 Prediction with Function Approximation
The simplest way to combine temporal-difference learning with approximation is to keep the TD target but optimize model parameters instead of table entries.
For example, with state-value prediction we may use a target:
\[
y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(S_{t+1}; w)
\]
and then update parameters to reduce the difference between the current prediction and that target.
In a linear model, the update may resemble stochastic gradient descent. In a neural network, the same principle applies, but the function class is much richer.
The crucial idea is that the Bellman logic remains. The representation changes, but the reinforcement learning objective is still driven by returns, value estimates, and recursive consistency.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
7.7 Control with Function Approximation
We can extend the same idea to action-value control. Instead of storing a tabular Q-function, we approximate:
\[
\hat{Q}(s, a; w)
\]
Then we can construct targets that resemble Q-learning:
\[
y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(S_{t+1}, a'; w)
\]
and update parameters so that \(\hat{Q}(S_t, A_t; w)\) moves toward \(y_t\).
Conceptually, this is a direct continuation of tabular Q-learning. But in practice, the combination of three ingredients can be unstable:

Bootstrapping.
Off-policy learning.
Function approximation.

This combination is so important that it is sometimes called the “deadly triad” in reinforcement learning. It explains why naive Q-learning with nonlinear approximators can behave poorly.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
section: 7.8 Why Neural Networks Enter the Picture
text:
7.8 Why Neural Networks Enter the Picture
If linear approximation already offers generalization, why do we need deep learning?
The answer is representation power. Linear models depend heavily on feature design. If the right features are not provided, the model may fail even when the task is learnable. In complex domains such as images, raw sensory streams, or large structured observations, manual feature design is often insufficient or too expensive.
Neural networks allow the agent to learn representations automatically. Rather than hand-crafting features for every state, we train a network to extract patterns that are useful for predicting values or selecting actions.
This is the core promise of deep reinforcement learning:
Learn representations and decision-making signals together.
But this promise comes with a cost. Neural networks are flexible, high-capacity function approximators, which also makes them harder to stabilize under reinforcement learning objectives.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
section: 7.9 The Difference Between Historical and Modern Practice
text:
7.9 The Difference Between Historical and Modern Practice
Older code examples often demonstrate the right algorithmic skeleton but use tools and assumptions that no longer match common modern workflows. You may see:

TensorFlow 1.x graph construction.
Older Gym APIs.
Minimal replay buffer implementations.
Limited experiment tracking.
Little attention to evaluation across random seeds.

These examples are still worth reading, especially for conceptual clarity. But a modern practitioner should separate the enduring ideas from the outdated tooling.
The enduring ideas include:

Temporal-difference targets.
Approximate value functions.
Exploration strategies.
Target computation for control.

The tooling details, however, should be updated when building serious systems today.
The same caution applies inside this project. The maintained approximation examples are honest bridge examples, not claims that shallow feature engineering is the best practical solution for these tasks. Their real value is explanatory:

they preserve the Bellman-style target clearly,
they show where handcrafted representation enters,
and they expose, at a small scale, why approximation makes learning more sensitive.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation
text:
7.10 The Road to DQN
Deep Q-Networks, or DQN, were historically important because they showed that Q-learning-style updates could be combined with deep neural networks to learn directly from high-dimensional inputs such as Atari frames.
This was not merely “Q-learning plus a larger model.” Several stabilizing ideas were needed to make training work at all, especially:

Experience replay.
Target networks.
Careful preprocessing and training design.

The next chapter studies DQN in detail and explains why those additions were so important.
crumbs: 7  From Tabular Methods to Function Approximation

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.1 From Q-Learning to DQN
Deep Q-Networks, usually abbreviated DQN, mark one of the most influential turning points in reinforcement learning. The core idea is easy to state:
Use a neural network to approximate the Q-function in a Q-learning-style algorithm.
The reason this mattered is that tabular Q-learning cannot handle raw, high-dimensional observations such as images. DQN showed that with the right stabilizing techniques, value-based reinforcement learning could scale far beyond small tabular environments.
Recall the tabular Q-learning update:
\[
Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)\right]
\]
DQN keeps the same target structure, but replaces the table with a neural network:
\[
Q(s, a; \theta)
\]
where \(\theta\) denotes the network parameters.
Instead of directly overwriting a table entry, we now train the network so that its prediction \(Q(S_t, A_t; \theta)\) moves toward a target value.
This is often written as:
\[
y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a'; \theta^-)
\]
where \(\theta^-\) are parameters of a separate target network. We will explain that choice shortly.
Training then minimizes a loss such as:
\[
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\left(y_t - Q(S_t, A_t; \theta)\right)^2\right]
\]
This looks close to supervised learning, but the similarity is misleading. In supervised learning, targets are usually fixed labels from a dataset. In DQN, the targets are themselves generated from the agent’s evolving estimates.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
section: 8.2 Why Naive Deep Q-Learning Is Unstable
text:
8.2 Why Naive Deep Q-Learning Is Unstable
If we simply plug a neural network into Q-learning, training can become highly unstable.
There are several reasons:

Consecutive samples are strongly correlated.
The target depends on the same network that is being updated.
The method is off-policy.
The updates rely on bootstrapping.

This creates a feedback loop. The network predicts values, those predictions define the targets, and those targets immediately reshape the network again. Without extra care, the process can oscillate or diverge.
That is why DQN should be understood not only as a neural version of Q-learning, but as a stabilization package for deep value learning.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.3 Experience Replay
One of the most important DQN ideas is the replay buffer.
Instead of updating the network only from the newest transition, the agent stores transitions such as:
\[
(s, a, r, s', d)
\]
in a memory buffer. During training, it samples minibatches from this buffer.
This has several benefits:

It breaks the strong temporal correlation in consecutive experience.
It reuses past data more efficiently.
It makes the training distribution smoother and more diverse.

Replay turns reinforcement learning into something closer to stochastic optimization over a growing dataset. That shift is one reason deep value learning became much more workable.
Historically, even simple replay buffer implementations already showed the basic advantage. Modern systems may add prioritized sampling, larger buffers, or more careful data management, but the fundamental insight remains the same.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.4 Target Networks
The second major stabilization idea is the target network.
If the same parameters are used both to predict the current Q-value and to compute the target, then the optimization target changes too quickly. The system is trying to chase its own moving predictions.
DQN reduces this problem by maintaining a second set of parameters, often written as \(\theta^-\), which are copied from the main network only periodically or updated more slowly.
The target then becomes:
\[
y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a'; \theta^-)
\]
This simple idea makes a large practical difference. The target becomes more stable, which improves learning behavior.
Older implementations often copy the target network every fixed number of steps. Many modern methods still use that basic pattern, though details vary by algorithm and codebase.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.5 The DQN Training Loop
At a high level, DQN training follows a repeated loop:

Observe the current state.
Choose an action, often with \(\epsilon\)-greedy exploration.
Step the environment and collect the next transition.
Store the transition in replay memory.
Sample a minibatch from replay.
Build bootstrap targets with the target network.
Update the online Q-network by gradient descent.
Periodically update the target network.

This loop is conceptually simple once written out, but each component plays a role in keeping learning stable enough to proceed.
In the maintained code for this project, the main reference implementation is:

examples/deep_rl/dqn_cartpole.py

That file is intentionally small enough to be read end to end. If you want to connect the abstract DQN loop to concrete code, the main pieces are:

ReplayBuffer, which stores transitions.
QNetwork, which maps observations to one Q-value per action.
select_action(...), which implements \(\epsilon\)-greedy behavior.
optimize(...), which builds the bootstrap target and performs the gradient step.
evaluate_policy(...), which separates exploratory training behavior from greedy evaluation.

This chapter is easier to internalize if you treat those names as the executable version of the pseudocode.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.6 Input Representation and Atari
DQN became famous because of its Atari results. In that setting, the network processed stacks of image frames rather than low-dimensional hand-crafted state features.
That achievement is historically important because it demonstrated end-to-end value learning from high-dimensional sensory input. The network did not rely on a manually designed state table. It learned directly from a representation much closer to raw observation.
At the same time, it is important not to mythologize the result too much. DQN also depended on careful preprocessing, frame stacking, reward clipping, and other design choices. In reinforcement learning, seemingly small implementation decisions often matter a great deal.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
section: 8.7 What Old DQN Code Still Teaches
text:
8.7 What Old DQN Code Still Teaches
Some older DQN examples in this repository use TensorFlow 1.x and older Gym versions. Those implementations are outdated as software artifacts, but they still teach several enduring lessons:

The Q-network predicts one value per action.
A replay buffer stores transitions for later sampling.
A separate target network stabilizes bootstrap targets.
Exploration is usually handled by an \(\epsilon\)-greedy policy.

Those are the algorithmic ideas worth carrying forward. The exact framework syntax, environment API, and engineering style should be treated as historical rather than current.
At the same time, the maintained examples in this project now include a modern PyTorch CartPole DQN implementation and a matching Double DQN variant. Those files are intentionally smaller and cleaner than the legacy scripts so that readers can trace the training loop, replay buffer usage, and target-network logic without needing to decode TensorFlow 1.x graph code first.
The maintained value-based path in this repository now consists of:

examples/deep_rl/dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/double_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/dueling_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/prioritized_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/rainbowish_cartpole.py

These files are not identical in status.

The first two are the clearest starting points if the goal is to understand the core algorithm.
The next two add specific stabilization or representation ideas on top of that base.
The last one is intentionally described as “Rainbow-ish” rather than full Rainbow, because it combines several ideas without claiming to be a complete reproduction of the full Rainbow recipe.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.8 Main Weaknesses of Basic DQN
Basic DQN was a breakthrough, but it has important limitations.
First, it is mainly designed for discrete action spaces. The max over actions is manageable when the action set is small, but it becomes difficult in continuous control.
Second, it can overestimate action values because the same estimates are used inside a max operator. This issue motivated later methods such as Double DQN.
Third, performance can be highly sensitive to reward scaling, exploration schedules, replay design, target update frequency, and network architecture.
Fourth, DQN is sample-hungry. Learning strong behavior can require many environment interactions.
These limitations do not make DQN unimportant. On the contrary, they explain why so much later research built directly on it.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.9 Beyond Basic DQN
Several influential methods can be understood as refinements of DQN:

Double DQN reduces overestimation bias.
Dueling networks separate value and advantage structure.
Prioritized replay samples transitions more selectively.
Distributional methods model return distributions rather than only expectations.

These extensions improved performance and stability in many settings, but the basic DQN structure remains a reference point for value-based deep reinforcement learning.
In the code for this book, the DQN path is now represented by:

examples/deep_rl/dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/double_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/dueling_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/prioritized_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/rainbowish_cartpole.py

That pairing is useful because it makes the target-construction difference concrete. Basic DQN uses the target network both to select and evaluate the next greedy action. Double DQN instead uses the online network to choose the next action and the target network to evaluate that chosen action.
The broader family is also useful pedagogically because it shows how modern value-based reinforcement learning usually evolves in practice. Rarely does a project jump directly from tabular Q-learning to a fully elaborate agent. More often, it accumulates stabilizing ingredients one by one: first replay and target networks, then improved targets, then architectural changes, then improved sampling schemes.

title: 8  Deep Q-Networks
text:
8.10 Why DQN Still Matters
DQN still matters for at least three reasons.
First, it is the clearest bridge from classical Q-learning to modern deep reinforcement learning.
Second, it illustrates the practical consequences of the deadly triad: bootstrapping, off-policy learning, and function approximation. The replay buffer and target network are direct responses to those problems.
Third, it teaches a broader lesson that appears throughout DRL:
Good reinforcement learning algorithms are often not just mathematical objectives. They are objectives plus stabilization mechanisms.
The next chapter turns from value-based deep reinforcement learning to policy gradient methods, which optimize the policy more directly and open the door to actor-critic approaches.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.1 Why Study Dynamic Programming?
Dynamic programming is one of the cleanest entry points into reinforcement learning. It is not the most realistic setting for large-scale applications, because it assumes access to the full environment model. But it provides an ideal laboratory for understanding value functions, Bellman equations, and policy improvement.
In an MDP, if we know the transition dynamics and reward function, then we can often compute value functions by repeated updates rather than by pure trial-and-error interaction. This is the basic idea of dynamic programming.
Dynamic programming matters for three reasons.
Even when we cannot use dynamic programming directly, its logic remains everywhere in reinforcement learning. Temporal-difference learning uses Bellman-style targets. Q-learning uses an optimality backup that mirrors dynamic programming. DQN approximates those same updates with a neural network.
So while dynamic programming is not the final practical answer, it is one of the best conceptual starting points.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
It makes the Bellman equations operational.
It separates evaluation from improvement very clearly.
It provides the conceptual foundation for many later algorithms.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.2 Policy Evaluation
Suppose we are given a policy \(\pi\). Policy evaluation asks a single question:
How good is this policy?
More precisely, we want to compute the value function \(V^\pi(s)\) for each state. From the Bellman expectation equation, we know that:
\[
V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\sum_{s',r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right]
\]
This is a system of equations, one for each state. In small problems, we may solve it directly. More often, we use iterative policy evaluation:
\[
V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)\sum_{s',r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V_k(s')\right]
\]
The idea is simple. Start with an initial guess for the value function, often all zeros. Then repeatedly apply the Bellman update until the values stabilize.
Each update uses the current estimate \(V_k\) to produce a better estimate \(V_{k+1}\). Under standard conditions, this process converges to the true value function of the policy.
In the maintained code for this book, this idea appears directly in:

examples/tabular/policy_evaluation.py

The key function is evaluate_deterministic_policy(...). It does exactly what the equation above says: for each non-terminal state, it loops over actions and next states, computes the Bellman expectation backup, and repeats until the largest change across states falls below a tolerance threshold.
This is worth pausing over. In many books, iterative policy evaluation is presented only as a formula. In code, the crucial operational detail is the stopping condition. The implementation does not stop because it knows the true answer. It stops because successive sweeps stop changing the value function by much.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.3 Policy Improvement
Once we know how good a policy is, the next natural step is to improve it.
Suppose we already have \(V^\pi\). For each state, we can ask which action gives the highest expected return if we act once and then continue according to the old policy. That leads to a greedy improvement step:
\[
\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right]
\]
This gives a new policy \(\pi'\). A key result in reinforcement learning is the policy improvement theorem, which says that this greedy policy is at least as good as the original policy under suitable conditions.
This is the basic pattern:

Evaluate the current policy.
Improve the policy greedily with respect to the current value estimates.
Repeat.

That alternating structure is the heart of policy iteration.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.4 Policy Iteration
Policy iteration combines policy evaluation and policy improvement into a loop.
The procedure is:

Start with an initial policy.
Evaluate the policy to obtain \(V^\pi\).
Improve the policy greedily.
If the policy no longer changes, stop. Otherwise, repeat.

This method is elegant because it mirrors how we often think about learning in general. First understand the consequences of the current behavior, then update the behavior based on that understanding.
In small finite MDPs, policy iteration can converge surprisingly quickly. It is especially useful for grid worlds and textbook environments, where the transition model is available and easy to inspect.
The maintained implementation is:

examples/tabular/policy_iteration.py

There are two functions worth reading together:

run_policy_iteration(...)
improve_policy(...)

The first orchestrates the outer loop. The second is where the greedy improvement step actually happens. If you want to see the policy improvement theorem in code form, this file is one of the cleanest places in the project to do it. The code evaluates the current policy, then for each state compares all available actions under the current value estimates, and replaces the old action with the best one.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.5 Value Iteration
Policy iteration is conceptually clean, but full policy evaluation can be expensive. Value iteration compresses the evaluation and improvement steps into a single update.
Instead of fully evaluating the current policy, value iteration directly applies the Bellman optimality update:
\[
V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a \sum_{s',r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V_k(s')\right]
\]
This update says: if the goal is optimal behavior, then at each iteration we should back up the best action immediately.
After the value function converges, we can recover an optimal policy by choosing the greedy action in each state:
\[
\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} P(s', r \mid s, a)\left[r + \gamma V^*(s')\right]
\]
Value iteration is often simpler to implement than policy iteration, and it highlights the role of the Bellman optimality equation very clearly.
The maintained implementation is:

examples/tabular/value_iteration.py

Compared with policy iteration, the important conceptual difference in code is this: the update takes a max inside the sweep itself. In other words, policy improvement is no longer a separate outer step. The Bellman optimality operator is applied directly during value updates.
That difference is visible in run_value_iteration(...), where each state update computes the value of every possible action and immediately keeps only the largest one. After convergence, the script performs one more greedy pass to extract the policy explicitly.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.6 Synchronous and In-Place Updates
There are several ways to implement dynamic programming updates. In synchronous updates, every state is updated using values from the previous sweep. In in-place updates, the algorithm overwrites values as soon as they are computed, allowing later states in the same sweep to use fresher information.
This detail may seem minor, but it affects convergence speed. In-place updates often propagate useful information faster. More advanced planning methods build on this idea by choosing which states to update in more strategic ways.
The current tabular dynamic-programming examples in this project use straightforward sweep-based updates. That is a good choice for a foundation text. It keeps the Bellman logic visible and avoids burying the main idea under optimization tricks.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.7 The Limits of Dynamic Programming
Dynamic programming is powerful, but its assumptions are strong.
First, it requires a model of the environment. In many real-world problems, we do not know the transition probabilities or reward function exactly.
Second, it assumes the state space is manageable. If the number of states is huge, storing and updating a value for every state becomes infeasible.
Third, it depends on repeated sweeps over the entire state space. This is difficult in high-dimensional or continuous domains.
These limitations explain why dynamic programming is not the dominant method in modern large-scale reinforcement learning. Still, the logic of Bellman backups and iterative improvement survives in almost every major algorithm family.

title: 4  Dynamic Programming
text:
4.8 From Planning to Learning
Dynamic programming is often described as a planning method rather than a learning method. That distinction is useful:

Planning uses a known model to compute good behavior.
Learning uses experience to estimate what the model or values should be.

The next chapter makes this transition explicit. We will drop the assumption of a known model and ask how an agent can estimate value functions directly from sampled episodes and transitions. That leads to Monte Carlo methods and temporal-difference learning.
For this reason, dynamic programming should be read here less as a practical recipe and more as a conceptual template. The exact same Bellman logic that appears in these grid world scripts later reappears in TD learning, Q-learning, DQN, and even actor-critic critics. The implementations become more approximate, more statistical, and more fragile, but the underlying backup idea does not disappear.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.1 Why Learn the Policy Directly?
So far, the deep reinforcement learning methods in this book have followed the value-based path. We started from tabular Q-learning, moved to function approximation, and then studied DQN as a deep version of value-based control.
That path is powerful, but it is not the only one.
Policy gradient methods take a different approach. Instead of learning action values and then selecting actions indirectly, they optimize the policy itself. This shift changes the mathematical object we train, the kinds of action spaces we can handle naturally, and the kinds of stability problems we encounter.
In value-based methods, the policy is derived from value estimates. For example, in Q-learning or DQN, we estimate \(Q(s, a)\) and then choose actions by taking an argmax or by adding exploration.
This works well in many discrete-action problems, but it has limitations.
First, the argmax operation becomes awkward in continuous action spaces. If actions are continuous, finding the maximizing action at every step may be difficult or expensive.
Second, some tasks benefit from learning stochastic policies directly. In uncertain environments, partial observability, competitive settings, or environments with multiple near-optimal behaviors, a stochastic policy may be more natural than a deterministic one.
Third, direct policy optimization avoids some of the indirectness of value-based control. Rather than estimating action values and then extracting behavior, policy gradient methods optimize behavior itself.
This leads to a new parameterization:
\[
\pi_\theta(a \mid s)
\]
where \(\theta\) are the parameters of the policy network.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
section: 9.2 The Objective of Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.2 The Objective of Policy Gradient Methods
The goal is to maximize expected return under the policy. At a high level, we may write the objective as:
\[
J(\theta)
\]
where \(J\) measures how good the policy is in expectation.
The central question is:
How do we compute a gradient that tells us how to improve the policy parameters?
This is where the policy gradient theorem enters. The theorem gives a way to estimate \(\nabla_\theta J(\theta)\) using sampled trajectories, without needing to differentiate through the environment dynamics directly.
The deep idea is that if an action led to better-than-expected outcomes, then the policy should become more likely to take that action in similar states. If an action led to worse-than-expected outcomes, the policy should become less likely to take it.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.3 The Log-Probability Trick
One of the most important identities behind policy gradients is:
\[
\nabla_\theta \pi_\theta(a \mid s) = \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)
\]
This may look like a technical step, but it is what makes policy optimization practical.
Using this identity, we can derive estimators that scale the gradient of the log-probability of an action by a signal that measures how good that action turned out to be.
This leads to the core intuition:
Increase the probability of actions that lead to high return, and decrease the probability of actions that lead to low return.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.4 REINFORCE
The classic Monte Carlo policy gradient algorithm is REINFORCE. Its update has the form:
\[
\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha G_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(A_t \mid S_t)
\]
Here, \(G_t\) is the return observed after taking action \(A_t\) in state \(S_t\).
This update says:

If the return after an action is high, increase the log-probability of that action.
If the return is low, reduce its relative probability.

REINFORCE is appealing because it is conceptually direct. It does not require a value function in its simplest form. It uses sampled returns to improve the policy directly.
This makes REINFORCE an important baseline for understanding the entire policy optimization family.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.5 Why REINFORCE Is Noisy
REINFORCE is elegant, but it suffers from a serious practical problem:
Its gradient estimates can have very high variance.
There are several reasons for this:

Returns can be noisy across episodes.
