Credit assignment is difficult when rewards are delayed.
Full-trajectory returns may fluctuate heavily even when the policy changes only slightly.

As a result, learning can be unstable or slow. The algorithm points in the right direction on average, but the individual gradient estimates can be extremely noisy.
This is one reason why pure Monte Carlo policy gradients are often more useful as teaching tools or baseline methods than as the final answer in serious reinforcement learning systems.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.6 Baselines and Variance Reduction
One of the most important ideas in policy gradient methods is the use of a baseline.
Instead of weighting the gradient by the raw return \(G_t\), we subtract a baseline \(b(s)\):
\[
\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \left(G_t - b(S_t)\right)\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(A_t \mid S_t)
\]
If chosen properly, this does not change the expected gradient, but it can reduce variance substantially.
The intuition is simple. We do not only want to know whether a return was large in absolute terms. We want to know whether it was better or worse than some reasonable expectation.
That “better than expected” idea later becomes the advantage function, which is central to actor-critic methods.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.7 Policy Networks in Practice
In deep reinforcement learning, the policy is often represented by a neural network.
For discrete actions, the network may output logits or probabilities over actions. For continuous actions, it may output the parameters of a probability distribution, such as a Gaussian mean and variance.
The training signal then comes from log-probabilities of sampled actions multiplied by returns, advantages, or related estimates.
Older implementations often present this structure in a compact form:

The network produces action probabilities.
An action is sampled from that distribution.
The log-probability of the sampled action is recorded.
After rewards are collected, the policy parameters are updated using a weighted log-probability objective.

That structure remains conceptually correct even when the surrounding software stack is old.
In the maintained examples for this project, the cleanest reference for this idea is now a self-implemented PyTorch REINFORCE example for CartPole:

examples/deep_rl/reinforce_cartpole.py

It records action log-probabilities during an episode, computes discounted returns afterward, and then performs a return-weighted policy update. That example is intentionally kept as the simplest policy-gradient reference in the codebase.
That role should be read carefully. reinforce_cartpole.py is not presented here as the strongest maintained policy optimization baseline in the project. It is presented as the clearest local implementation of the policy-gradient idea itself. If the question is “what does a return-weighted log-probability update look like in code?”, this is the right file to read first.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.8 Strengths of Policy Gradient Methods
Policy gradient methods have several important strengths.
First, they support stochastic policies naturally.
Second, they handle continuous action spaces more gracefully than max-based value methods.
Third, they optimize the policy directly rather than relying on a value-to-policy extraction step.
Fourth, they provide a clean foundation for many later methods, including actor-critic algorithms, PPO, TRPO, and other modern policy optimization methods.
These strengths help explain why policy-based deep reinforcement learning became such a major branch of the field.
In the current code for this book, pure policy gradient is represented mainly by REINFORCE. More advanced policy optimization examples such as PPO are maintained separately and, at present, are backed by a mature third-party implementation rather than a from-scratch local reimplementation. That decision is practical: for this project, a stable and validated training baseline matters more than insisting that every policy method be hand-written first.
This means the codebase currently separates two goals that are often conflated:

a transparent local implementation that makes the principle easy to inspect,
and a stronger maintained baseline that is more reliable to run.

For policy gradients in this project, REINFORCE mainly serves the first role.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.9 Weaknesses of Pure Policy Gradients
At the same time, pure policy gradient methods also have clear weaknesses.

Gradient estimates can have high variance.
Learning may require many samples.
Credit assignment can still be difficult in long-horizon tasks.
Without value estimation, the algorithm may use data inefficiently.

These weaknesses point directly toward the next major idea:
Combine policy learning with value estimation.
That hybrid approach leads to actor-critic methods.

title: 9  Policy Gradient Methods
text:
9.10 Why Policy Gradients Matter
Policy gradients matter because they expand the reinforcement learning toolbox beyond greedy value maximization. They show that decision-making policies can be parameterized and optimized directly, which becomes especially important in continuous control and large-scale deep reinforcement learning.
They also introduce several ideas that will remain central:

Log-probability objectives.
Return-weighted policy updates.
Baselines for variance reduction.
The separation between policy optimization and value estimation.

The next chapter builds on these ideas by combining a policy learner with a value estimator in the actor-critic framework.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.1 The Main Idea
Policy gradient methods optimize the policy directly, which gives them flexibility and conceptual clarity. But pure policy gradients often suffer from high variance and poor sample efficiency. Value-based methods, on the other hand, use bootstrapping to learn more efficiently, but they may be less natural in continuous action settings and can be unstable in other ways.
Actor-critic methods combine the strengths of both approaches.
This is one of the most important design patterns in modern reinforcement learning.
An actor-critic method has two components:
The actor decides what to do. The critic evaluates those decisions and provides a learning signal.
This division of labor is powerful because it separates two difficult problems:
In deep reinforcement learning, both actor and critic are often neural networks, though their exact forms vary across algorithms.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
The actor, which represents the policy.
The critic, which estimates how good states, actions, or decisions are.




How to choose actions.
How to judge whether those actions were good.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.2 Why Add a Critic?
The main reason to add a critic is variance reduction and improved learning efficiency.
Instead of waiting for a full Monte Carlo return as in REINFORCE, the critic can provide a learned estimate of future return. That estimate becomes a stronger, more immediate training signal for the actor.
In its simplest form, the actor update may look like:
\[
\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \hat{A}_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(A_t \mid S_t)
\]
where \(\hat{A}_t\) is an estimate of the advantage of the selected action.
This already shows the core relationship:
The actor changes the policy, and the critic supplies the signal that says whether the chosen action was better or worse than expected.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.3 The Advantage Function
The advantage function is one of the central ideas in actor-critic methods. It is defined as:
\[
A^\pi(s, a) = Q^\pi(s, a) - V^\pi(s)
\]
It measures how much better or worse an action is relative to the state’s average value under the current policy.
This is useful because raw returns alone do not tell us whether an action was surprisingly good. A large return may simply reflect that the state itself was favorable. The advantage function tries to isolate the contribution of the action relative to the baseline expectation.
This is precisely the kind of signal that helps reduce variance in policy updates.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.4 Temporal-Difference Critics
Many actor-critic methods use TD learning for the critic. For example, the critic may update a state-value estimate using:
\[
V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left(R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\right)
\]
The TD error
\[
\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)
\]
often serves a dual role:

It updates the critic.
It acts as a policy learning signal for the actor.

This is elegant because the same one-step surprise signal helps both components learn together.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.5 A Basic Actor-Critic Update
A simple actor-critic algorithm might follow this loop:

Observe state \(S_t\).
Sample action \(A_t\) from the actor.
Receive reward \(R_{t+1}\) and next state \(S_{t+1}\).
Compute a TD-style target for the critic.
Compute a TD error or advantage estimate.
Update the critic toward the target.
Update the actor using the advantage-weighted log-probability gradient.

This structure is one of the cleanest examples of how reinforcement learning combines prediction and control in a single learning system.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.6 Synchronous and Asynchronous Variants
Historically, actor-critic ideas appeared in several forms, including synchronous and asynchronous training.
One notable family was A3C (Asynchronous Advantage Actor-Critic), where multiple workers interacted with environments in parallel and updated shared global parameters. This approach helped reduce correlation in experience and made training more efficient at the time.
Older repositories often contain A3C-style implementations with threaded workers, local copies of policy and value networks, and periodic synchronization. Even though the software style may look dated, the conceptual lesson remains important:
Parallel data collection and advantage-based policy updates can make actor-critic methods much more practical.
Later systems often moved toward synchronous batched training, but the actor-critic principle remained intact.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
section: 10.7 A2C and the Simplification of A3C
text:
10.7 A2C and the Simplification of A3C
A2C (Advantage Actor-Critic) is often described as a synchronous counterpart to A3C. Instead of many asynchronous workers updating parameters independently, data is often collected in parallel and then used in more coordinated batch updates.
This simplification can make implementations easier to reason about and debug. It also aligns better with modern accelerator-based training workflows.
The deeper point is not the naming difference between A2C and A3C. The deeper point is that both rely on the same core structure:

A policy network proposes actions.
A value estimator supplies a baseline or advantage signal.
The actor is updated in the direction of actions that appear better than expected.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.8 Why Actor-Critic Became Central
Actor-critic methods became central because they occupy a strong middle ground.
Compared with pure policy gradients, they usually reduce variance and improve sample efficiency.
Compared with purely value-based methods, they often handle stochastic or continuous policies more naturally.
Compared with pure Monte Carlo methods, they can learn incrementally through bootstrapping.
This balance explains why so many modern deep reinforcement learning algorithms are actor-critic variants in one form or another.
It also explains why actor-critic is harder to implement robustly than its pseudocode may suggest. Once a policy update depends on a learned value signal, the system becomes a coupled optimization problem. The actor depends on the critic for guidance, and the critic depends on data generated by the actor. That feedback loop is powerful, but it is also where many implementation failures come from.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.9 Remaining Challenges
Actor-critic methods are powerful, but they are not automatically stable.
They inherit complexity from both sides:

The actor can drift too quickly.
The critic can become inaccurate or biased.
Bootstrapping can still create instability.
Off-policy variants can become especially delicate.

In practice, a weak critic can damage the actor badly, because the policy update depends on the critic’s signal. This creates a feedback loop similar in spirit to the issues seen in deep value learning.
That is why later actor-critic methods often include additional design choices such as clipping, trust-region style constraints, entropy regularization, target networks, replay buffers, or delayed updates.

title: 10  Actor-Critic Methods
text:
10.10 From Actor-Critic to Modern DRL
Actor-critic is less a single algorithm than a broad framework. Many influential deep reinforcement learning methods can be understood as actor-critic descendants or refinements.
Examples include:

A2C and A3C.
DDPG for continuous control.
TD3 as a refinement of deterministic actor-critic ideas.
SAC as an entropy-regularized actor-critic method.
PPO as a practical policy optimization method built around actor-critic structure.

This is why actor-critic deserves special attention. It is one of the most fertile templates in the entire field.
In the maintained code for this book, actor-critic ideas are currently represented in two different ways.
First, the simplest policy-gradient baseline remains a local self-implementation:

examples/deep_rl/reinforce_cartpole.py

Second, the main actor-critic training path is currently represented by stable library-backed LunarLander examples:

examples/deep_rl/actor_critic_lunarlander.py
examples/deep_rl/a2c_lunarlander.py
examples/deep_rl/ppo_lunarlander.py

These files use Stable-Baselines3 rather than local from-scratch implementations. That choice is deliberate. In this project, the actor-critic family proved much more sensitive to implementation details than the value-based DQN family, so the maintained baseline now prioritizes validated training behavior over hand-written minimalism.
From a conceptual perspective, this is still appropriate. The point of this chapter is to understand the actor-critic pattern: policy updates guided by a learned value-based signal. The specific software boundary between “written locally” and “delegated to a stable library” is secondary to that core idea.
Still, the implementation boundary should be stated explicitly because it affects how the code should be used:

reinforce_cartpole.py is the local conceptual baseline for direct policy gradients.
ppo_lunarlander.py is currently the strongest maintained actor-critic-family baseline in the repository.
actor_critic_lunarlander.py and a2c_lunarlander.py preserve the algorithmic positions in the codebase, but they should be read with the same practical caution used elsewhere in this book: an algorithm name in the repository is not automatically a claim that every variant is equally validated.

The next chapter surveys more advanced deep reinforcement learning methods and shows how they extend the ideas introduced so far.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.1 Double DQN
By now, the main structural ideas of deep reinforcement learning are in place. We have seen value-based learning through DQN, direct policy optimization through policy gradients, and hybrid learning through actor-critic methods.
This chapter surveys several advanced methods that refine those foundations. The goal is not to turn the chapter into a catalog of every algorithm in the literature. Instead, the goal is to show how later methods respond to specific weaknesses in earlier ones.
That perspective is important. Most advanced DRL methods are best understood not as isolated inventions, but as answers to concrete problems:
Basic DQN tends to overestimate action values because the same estimates are used both to select and to evaluate the maximizing action. This issue arises from the max operator in the bootstrap target.
Double DQN reduces this bias by decoupling action selection from action evaluation. In one common form, the online network selects the action, while the target network evaluates it.
The key idea is not complicated:
This helps reduce optimistic value estimates and often improves learning stability.
Double DQN is a good example of a broader pattern in DRL: small changes to target construction can matter a great deal.
In this repository, that idea is now captured directly by comparing:
The architectural skeleton is almost the same. The important change is in the target: the online network selects the next action, while the target network evaluates that action.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
Choose the best next action using one network.
Evaluate that chosen action using another network.





examples/deep_rl/dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/double_dqn_cartpole.py

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.2 Dueling Networks
Another extension of DQN is the dueling network architecture.
Instead of predicting action values in a single undifferentiated stream, the network separates:

A state-value stream.
An advantage stream.

These are then combined to produce action-value estimates.
The motivation is that in many states, it is useful to estimate how good the state is even when the exact choice among similar actions matters less. This architectural decomposition can improve representation learning and make value estimation more efficient in some settings.
Dueling networks also show that progress in DRL often comes not only from new loss functions, but from better structural biases in the function approximator itself.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.3 Prioritized Replay
Standard replay buffers sample transitions uniformly. But not all transitions are equally informative. Some contain large prediction errors or learning opportunities that may deserve more attention.
Prioritized experience replay changes the sampling distribution so that more informative transitions are replayed more often.
This can improve learning efficiency, but it also introduces new issues:

The sample distribution becomes biased.
Importance weighting or correction terms may be needed.
The system becomes more sensitive to replay design.

This is another recurring DRL lesson: better sample usage often comes at the cost of more complicated training dynamics.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.4 Deterministic Policy Gradients and DDPG
Policy gradients naturally support continuous action spaces, but stochastic policy methods are not the only option. In some settings, a deterministic policy can be more efficient:
\[
a = \mu_\theta(s)
\]
This idea leads to deterministic policy gradient methods and, in deep reinforcement learning, to DDPG (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient).
DDPG can be viewed as a continuous-control actor-critic method with several DQN-like ingredients:

A replay buffer.
Target networks.
An actor network for actions.
A critic network for action values.

This hybrid structure made DDPG historically important. It brought together value-based stabilization ideas and actor-critic structure in continuous action settings.
However, DDPG is also known for being sensitive and sometimes brittle in practice. Its importance lies both in what it achieved and in what later methods learned from its weaknesses.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.5 TD3
TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) was designed in part to address weaknesses in DDPG.
Its main ideas include:

Using two critics to reduce overestimation bias.
Delaying actor updates relative to critic updates.
Adding smoothing noise in target action evaluation.

The broader lesson is familiar by now: once a method becomes unstable or biased, later work often improves it by adding redundancy, delay, or smoothing to the target-generation process.
TD3 is a strong example of how practical deep reinforcement learning often advances through careful control of approximation error rather than through entirely new conceptual frameworks.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.6 SAC
Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) is one of the most influential modern actor-critic methods for continuous control.
Its defining idea is to optimize not only expected return, but also policy entropy. Informally, SAC encourages the policy to remain appropriately stochastic while still pursuing reward.
This leads to an objective that values both:

High reward.
High entropy.

Entropy regularization can improve exploration and make training more robust. SAC also uses off-policy learning and critic-based updates, making it both powerful and relatively sample-efficient compared with many earlier policy-gradient methods.
SAC is important because it shows that exploration does not always need to be treated as an external add-on like \(\epsilon\)-greedy noise. It can be built directly into the optimization objective.
In the current repository, the continuous-control positions for DDPG, TD3, and SAC are represented by local Pendulum scripts. At the time of writing, these files have shown clear learning behavior in manual project runs, so they can now be treated as validated self-implemented references for the continuous-control portion of the codebase. Even so, it is worth keeping the level of the claim precise: this is a practical project-level validation, not a claim of benchmark-grade evaluation across many seeds and configurations.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.7 PPO
While DQN-style methods and deterministic actor-critic methods each solve certain problems well, many practitioners also need a robust and practical policy optimization algorithm that works across a wide range of tasks.
PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) became popular largely because it is relatively simple, strong in practice, and easier to implement reliably than some earlier trust-region methods.
The core PPO idea is to update the policy while limiting how far it moves in a single step. In practice, this is often done through a clipped objective that discourages excessively large policy changes.
The intuition is straightforward:

Improve the policy.
Do not let the update change behavior too abruptly.

This helps stabilize training. PPO is often used with actor-critic structure, advantage estimates, and minibatch optimization over collected rollouts.
Although PPO is not the final answer for every problem, it became a practical default in many settings because it sits in a useful balance between sophistication and implementation simplicity.
In the maintained code for this project, PPO is currently represented by:

examples/deep_rl/ppo_lunarlander.py

This example is backed by Stable-Baselines3 rather than a local from-scratch implementation. That choice reflects a practical lesson from the project: for modern on-policy methods, a mature implementation can be far more reliable than a compact hand-written reimplementation that only looks correct on paper. In other words, the algorithmic idea still belongs in the book, but the maintained code path now favors a verified PPO baseline over a fragile minimal script.
At the time of writing, this is also the most reliable maintained policy-optimization baseline in the repository. That does not make PPO universally superior to every other actor-critic-family method. It means only that, in this codebase, PPO on LunarLander is currently the clearest place where the underlying ideas and practical training behavior meet in a way that has actually been checked.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.8 Distributional and Rainbow-Style Ideas
Another important direction in value-based deep reinforcement learning is distributional RL. Instead of predicting only the expected return, distributional methods model a distribution over possible returns.
This shift provides richer learning signals and can improve performance in some settings.
When several improvements to DQN are combined, including Double DQN, dueling networks, prioritized replay, multi-step returns, and distributional ideas, the resulting system is often described in the spirit of Rainbow.
This is a useful reminder that modern DRL performance often depends on layered combinations of improvements rather than a single trick.
In the maintained examples for this project, the DQN refinement path now includes:

examples/deep_rl/dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/double_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/dueling_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/prioritized_dqn_cartpole.py
examples/deep_rl/rainbowish_cartpole.py

The final script is intentionally described as “Rainbow-ish” rather than a full Rainbow reproduction. It combines several major value-based refinements without claiming to be a complete benchmark-grade Rainbow implementation.
That naming discipline is important. Advanced reinforcement learning code often accumulates features gradually, and it is easy to overstate what has actually been implemented. In this book, the better habit is to describe an implementation as precisely as possible, even when the precise description is less glamorous than the paper title that inspired it.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
section: 11.9 How to Organize the Algorithm Landscape
text:
11.9 How to Organize the Algorithm Landscape
At this stage, the algorithm landscape can start to feel crowded. A useful way to stay organized is to group methods by the question they are trying to answer.
Value-based refinements:

How can we estimate action values more accurately and more stably?

Policy optimization refinements:

How can we improve the policy without making destructive updates?

Actor-critic refinements:

How can the actor and critic support one another without creating unstable feedback loops?

Exploration and regularization refinements:

How can we encourage useful exploration and robust optimization?

Once you see methods through those lenses, the field becomes less like a list of names and more like a sequence of design responses to recurring problems.

title: 11  Advanced Deep Reinforcement Learning
text:
11.10 A Practical Interpretation of “Advanced”
In deep reinforcement learning, “advanced” does not simply mean mathematically more complicated. Often it means:

Better control of bias and variance.
Better optimization stability.
Better use of data.
Better support for large or continuous action spaces.
Better robustness to implementation details.

This is worth emphasizing because the field can otherwise feel too paper-driven. In practice, advanced methods matter when they improve the reliability of training, not only when they introduce new notation.
That is also the perspective taken in this repository. An “advanced” method is not automatically promoted to a main reference just because it is newer. It becomes central only when its role in the algorithmic story is clear and its maintained code path is honest about its implementation status.
The next chapter turns from algorithm families to practice. There we focus on the engineering and experimental discipline required to train, evaluate, and debug deep reinforcement learning systems responsibly.

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.1 Why Practice Is Harder Than the Pseudocode
Understanding algorithms is necessary, but it is not sufficient for doing deep reinforcement learning well. In practice, many DRL failures are not caused by misunderstanding the headline idea of an algorithm. They come from unstable training, poor evaluation, weak baselines, reward design mistakes, or incorrect experimental assumptions.
This chapter focuses on the practical side of deep reinforcement learning. The goal is to explain what it takes to move from “I know the algorithm name” to “I can train, inspect, and evaluate a DRL system responsibly.”
That makes this chapter a fitting conclusion to the book. The earlier chapters introduced the objects, equations, and algorithm families. This chapter asks a stricter question:
What habits are required if we want those ideas to survive contact with real code and real experiments?
Reinforcement learning pseudocode often looks short and clean. A paper may describe an elegant update rule in a few lines. But real training systems contain many moving parts:
Each of these can influence outcomes. This is why two implementations of the “same” algorithm may perform very differently.
Deep reinforcement learning is unusually sensitive to hidden details. Good practice begins with respecting that sensitivity. One of the themes of this book has been that reinforcement learning should not be reduced to slogans like “just use DQN” or “just use PPO.” Practice begins when we admit that the result depends on much more than the algorithm name.

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.2 Environment Design and Task Framing
Before training begins, the problem itself must be framed carefully.
Important questions include:

What is the observation given to the agent?
What actions are available?
What counts as episode termination?
Is the reward dense, sparse, or shaped?
What behavior actually reflects success?

These questions matter because reinforcement learning agents optimize what they are given, not what the designer intended implicitly.
If the reward is misaligned with the real goal, the agent may learn behavior that looks successful numerically while failing conceptually. This is often described as reward hacking or specification gaming.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.3 Reward Design
Reward design is one of the most delicate parts of reinforcement learning.
A reward should encourage the intended long-term behavior, but that is often easier said than done. Rewards that are too sparse may make learning impossible. Rewards that are too dense or overly shaped may teach the wrong shortcut. A badly chosen penalty may dominate the learning signal and suppress useful exploration.
Good reward design usually involves several habits:

Start with the simplest meaningful objective.
Inspect trajectories rather than relying only on scalar reward curves.
Look for unintended exploitative behavior.
Distinguish between progress signals and final objectives.

In many projects, the reward function is not a small detail. It is the central design decision. A weak reward can make a good algorithm look useless. A misleading reward can make a bad policy look successful.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.4 Exploration in Practice
Exploration is easy to mention and hard to get right.
Different algorithm families handle it differently:

Value-based methods often rely on explicit exploration rules such as \(\epsilon\)-greedy behavior.
Stochastic policy methods explore through action sampling.
Entropy-regularized methods incorporate exploration pressure into the objective itself.
Continuous-control methods may inject noise into actions or policy parameters.

The right exploration strategy depends on the task. Sparse-reward problems are especially sensitive. In such settings, a nominally correct algorithm can fail simply because the agent never encounters informative experience.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.5 Monitoring Training
A deep reinforcement learning system should not be judged only by a single reward plot.
Good monitoring often includes:

Episode returns over time.
Running averages across windows of episodes.
Value loss and policy loss trends.
Entropy or exploration-related statistics.
Episode length.
Replay-buffer or rollout statistics.
Periodic evaluation returns under a fixed policy.

These signals help answer different questions. A rising reward curve may still hide critic collapse, policy saturation, or unstable exploration. Logging multiple views of the training process makes it easier to diagnose failure modes early.
Older codebases often include only minimal reward plots. They are useful, but modern practice usually needs a richer experimental record.
This point is not abstract in this project. Some of the most useful debugging progress came only after separating:

training returns under exploratory behavior,
periodic evaluation returns under a fixed policy,
and, in some cases, the best checkpoint seen during training from the final checkpoint at the end.

That distinction turned out to matter especially in the deep examples. A training curve alone can make an implementation look healthy even when the evaluation policy is weak or unstable.
This is one of the practical lessons that is easy to miss in a theory-first presentation. Reinforcement learning logs do not interpret themselves. A curve is evidence, not a verdict.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
section: 12.6 Evaluation and the Problem of Random Seeds
text:
12.6 Evaluation and the Problem of Random Seeds
Evaluation in reinforcement learning is harder than many newcomers expect.
A single training run is rarely enough evidence. Performance can vary substantially across:

Random initialization.
Environment stochasticity.
Exploration noise.
Replay-buffer contents.
Hardware and implementation details.

For this reason, it is good practice to evaluate across multiple random seeds and report summary statistics rather than only the best run.
This matters especially when comparing algorithms. A method that looks better in one seed may be worse on average. Without repeated runs, it is easy to over-interpret noise.
In this repository, that lesson should be taken literally. Some examples were first judged by a single run and later turned out to be weaker than they appeared. Others looked mediocre early in training and only became convincing after longer runs. So when reading results in this project, it is important to distinguish among three different claims:

the code is syntactically correct,
the code shows a learning trend in one manual run,
the code has been manually validated enough to be treated as a recommended baseline.

Those are not the same thing. A foundation text should be especially careful here, because readers often remember the confidence of a statement longer than the conditions under which it was true.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
section: 12.7 Hyperparameters Matter More Than People Expect
text:
12.7 Hyperparameters Matter More Than People Expect
Deep reinforcement learning is highly sensitive to hyperparameters. Important choices may include:

Learning rates.
Discount factor.
Replay-buffer size.
Batch size.
Target update frequency.
Entropy coefficient.
Exploration schedule.
Network size and normalization strategy.

This does not mean every project requires an enormous hyperparameter search. But it does mean that weak results are not always conceptual failures. Sometimes the algorithm is fine and the training setup is not.
One practical skill in DRL is learning to distinguish “the method is wrong for this task” from “the configuration is preventing the method from learning.” That distinction saves enormous time and prevents many false conclusions.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.8 Debugging Deep Reinforcement Learning
Debugging DRL is often a layered process.
Start with the simplest checks:

Does the environment return sensible observations, rewards, and termination signals?
Are actions within the valid range?
Are rewards exploding, vanishing, or using the wrong sign?
Is the replay buffer or rollout collector storing the right transitions?
Are tensor shapes and masks correct?

Then move to algorithmic checks:

Are targets computed as intended?
Is the actor actually improving according to the critic?
Is the critic learning anything meaningful?
Is the entropy collapsing too early?
Are gradients exploding or becoming zero?

Finally, inspect behavior directly. Watching episodes is often one of the fastest ways to catch errors that are invisible in logs.
This is worth emphasizing because deep reinforcement learning can otherwise become too abstract. If an agent’s behavior looks obviously wrong, no amount of elegant notation should persuade us that the system is healthy.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.9 Reproducibility and Experiment Discipline
Good reinforcement learning practice depends heavily on experiment discipline.
Useful habits include:

Fix random seeds when debugging.
Save model checkpoints regularly.
Record configuration files or command-line arguments.
Keep training and evaluation settings clearly separated.
Log software versions and environment variants.
Avoid changing many variables at once.

These habits sound mundane, but they save enormous time. In reinforcement learning, a result that cannot be reproduced is often not a result you can trust.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.10 Choosing the Right Algorithm Family
In practice, the “best” algorithm depends on the task.
If the action space is small and discrete, value-based methods may be attractive.
If the action space is continuous, policy-based or actor-critic methods are often more natural.
If robustness and practical ease matter, methods such as PPO are often popular.
If sample efficiency matters strongly and the task fits off-policy assumptions, methods such as SAC or TD3 may be appealing.
This is why algorithm choice should be framed as problem matching rather than prestige ranking. The most advanced method on paper is not automatically the best choice for a given project.
This repository itself is a useful case study in that principle.

For tabular reasoning, the smallest grid-world scripts are the best references.
For value-based deep reinforcement learning, the maintained PyTorch DQN family is the cleanest locally implemented path.
For policy-gradient methods, the most reliable maintained baseline in the current project is examples/deep_rl/ppo_lunarlander.py, which is backed by Stable-Baselines3 rather than a local from-scratch implementation.
For continuous-control actor-critic methods such as DDPG, TD3, and SAC, the repository now contains local implementations that have shown clear learning behavior in manual Pendulum runs, so they can be treated as validated project examples, though not as fully benchmarked final word implementations.

That last point is important. A method should not be judged better merely because it was reimplemented locally. If a mature third-party implementation is the only version that has currently been validated as reliable in the project, then that is the version that should be treated as the practical reference.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
text:
12.11 Working with Historical Code
This repository includes older reinforcement learning implementations. They are valuable for learning, but they should be handled with care in practice.
They often preserve the algorithmic skeleton clearly:

Data collection.
Target construction.
Policy or value updates.
Logging over episodes.

But they may also rely on:

Outdated framework APIs.
Old environment interfaces.
Minimal safety checks.
Weak experiment tracking.
Training conventions that are no longer standard.

A good way to use such code is to extract the enduring algorithmic logic while updating the software engineering around it.
The same principle also applies to comparing local implementations with third-party baselines. In this repository, some actor-critic-family methods were difficult to stabilize as local minimal implementations. Rather than pretending otherwise, the maintained policy-gradient path now distinguishes clearly between:

self-implemented conceptual references, such as examples/deep_rl/reinforce_cartpole.py,
and third-party-backed practical baselines, such as examples/deep_rl/ppo_lunarlander.py.

That distinction is not a weakness in the book. It is part of good scientific hygiene. A foundation text should explain principles clearly, but it should also report honestly what has and has not been validated in the actual codebase it accompanies.
crumbs: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice

title: 12  Deep Reinforcement Learning in Practice
section: 12.12 What Good DRL Practice Looks Like
text:
12.12 What Good DRL Practice Looks Like
Strong deep reinforcement learning practice is not defined by memorizing many paper names. It is defined by a combination of conceptual clarity and experimental discipline.
At a practical level, that usually means:

Framing the environment carefully.
Designing the reward thoughtfully.
Monitoring the right signals.
Evaluating across multiple runs.
Debugging systematically.
Matching the algorithm to the task.

If those habits are in place, even a relatively simple algorithm can teach a great deal. If they are absent, even a sophisticated method can produce misleading results.
This is the final practical message of the book:

learn the mathematical objects clearly,
connect them to code concretely,
and evaluate claims with discipline rather than enthusiasm.

If those three habits are maintained, the field becomes much less mysterious. Reinforcement learning is still difficult, but it stops being magical. It becomes a body of ideas, approximations, and engineering decisions that can be studied, tested, and improved honestly.
This completes the main arc of the book. We began with the foundations of reinforcement learning, moved through classical methods, then entered deep reinforcement learning through function approximation, DQN, policy gradients, actor-critic methods, advanced DRL variants, and practical training considerations. The intended result is not only familiarity with algorithm names. It is a durable mental model of how the pieces fit together and how to judge real implementations without self-deception.

title: 2  Reinforcement Learning Overview
section: 2.9 How to Read This Book with the Code
text:
2.9 How to Read This Book with the Code
This book is meant to be read together with the maintained code in examples/. The goal is not to treat the code as a decorative appendix. The goal is to use the code to make the ideas operational.
The structure of the repository mirrors the conceptual arc of the book:

examples/bandits/ covers the simplest exploration problems.
examples/tabular/ covers dynamic programming, Monte Carlo prediction, TD learning, SARSA, and Q-learning.
examples/approximation/ shows the bridge from tables to feature-based value approximation.
examples/deep_rl/ contains the neural-network-based examples.

That structure matters for reading. If a chapter introduces a Bellman backup, you should be able to find a concrete loop in the corresponding script where that backup is computed. If a chapter distinguishes on-policy from off-policy learning, you should be able to locate the exact line where one method uses the actually chosen next action and another uses a greedy max.
This is also the point where honesty matters. Not every maintained example in this repository has the same status.

Some examples are small, self-contained, and intended as direct principle-first references. The tabular scripts and the PyTorch DQN family mostly fit this category.
Some examples are useful conceptual bridges but are more sensitive in practice. The function-approximation scripts belong here.
Some policy-gradient examples use a mature third-party implementation rather than a local from-scratch reimplementation. That is a deliberate choice in this project, because for some actor-critic-family methods the stable engineering recipe matters as much as the headline formula.

The right way to use this book, then, is not to assume that every chapter points to code of the same kind. The right way is to ask, chapter by chapter:

What is the core principle?
Where does that principle appear in code?
Is this code meant as a minimal transparent implementation, a practical baseline, or a maintained third-party-backed reference?

The next chapter introduces the mathematical framework that organizes these ideas: the Markov decision process.
